California
Fair P01itical
Practices Commission
Apr j.I 19, 19 91
John R. Shaw, City Attorney
city of San Juan capistrano
32400 Paseo Adelanto
San Juan Capistrano, CA 92675
Re: Your Request for Advice
our File No. r-91--126
Dear Mr. Shaw:
You are seeking advice as City Attorney of San Juan
capistrano city Attorney regardinq your duties and
responsibilities under the con f I ict-o f- interest provisions of the
Polltical 
Reform Act (the rraqgrrl.l We provide you with advice
pursuant to Regulation 18329 (copy enclosed). Because your
question does not reference a specific pending governmental
decision, the advice proviced in tnis lLtter is informal onJ.y.2
The following advice is based upon the facts provided in your
Ietter of March 1, 1991, telephone conversations with you on March
l?, LggL and vrith Mr. Tom Tomlinson of the city's planning
department on March 7, Lgg]- and a letter dated March 12, 1991 fron
Mr. Tomlinson in response to this office's request for additional
i n f orrnat ion .
OUESTI ON
Forthcoming decision before the city's planning conmission
concern a propoied 6o-Iot subdivision six-tenths of a ni1e, or
3,168 feet, fiom your residence. As city attorney may you advise
the planning commission regarding various questions that may
arisl, including the subdivision tentative map?
1 Gor"r.,*"nt code sections 81ooo-9L015. A11 statutory references
are to the Government code unless otherwise indicated. 
commission
regulatj.ons appear at 2 california code of Regulations Section
18ooo, et seo. AlL references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the Californj.a Code of Regulations'
2 Inforrnal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
imnunity provj'ded by an opinion or formal written advice'
lcovernrnent code Seition 83114; 2 CaJ" code of Regs'
Section r8329 (c) (3) . )
428J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o sacramento cA 958O4-O8O7 . (916)322'5660


File No. 1-9L-726
Page 2
CONCLUS TON
You may provide advice to the planning commission provided
the decisions on the proposed subdivision $ri11 not have a
foreseeabl,e material financial. effect on your property which is
distinguishable fron the effect on the public generally.
FACTS
The City of San Juan capistrano planning comtnj-ssion is
scheduled to hold its first public hearing on a proposed tentative
subdivision map for a 50-lot proposed subdivision. Your residence
is located witnin a subdivision adjacent to the subdivision site,
approxirnately six-tenths of a mile, or 3,168 feet, from the
neirest proposed subdivision boundary Iine.
As city attorney you are frequently cal-Ied upon to provide
Iegal advice to the planning commission. You anticipate that
IegaI questions may arj.se concerning the processinq of the
=uudivi=ion tentative map at the time that the subdivision project
comes before the planning commission. It is possible that other
decisions involving the proposed subdivision may require your
Lnput as we).J..
ANALYSIS
A public official 
may not make, participate in, or use.his or
her official 
position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official 
knows or has reason to know he or she has a
financial interest. 
(section a71oo.) A public official 
has a
f inancia.L interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeabJ.e
that the decision wiII have a material iinancial effect,
distinguishable from i.ts effect on the public generally, on the
of f j.ciiI, 
a member of official's 
immediate family, or on any real
property in which the of f ici.al has a direct or indj.rect interest
iortn one thousand (51,ooo) or more. (Section 87103 (b) ' )
As city attorney you are a public official' 
(section 82048')
If your intlrest in your property is worth more than one thousand
doliars, you must thus dilqualify yourself fron participating in
decisions- concerning the pioposed subdivision which wiII have a
reasonably foreseeabLe material financial effect on you or your
real propLrty interests that is d i sti ngui,shable fron the effect on
the public generally. 
(Section 87103 (b) ' )
Foreseeable Uaterial Financial Effect
WhenthereisaSubstantiallikelihoodthattheeffectofa
decision will occur, it is reasonably foreseeable' while
certaintyisnotrequired,aneffectthatismerelyapossibility
is not rlasonably fdreseeabLe. (Dorrnev Cares v. Downev Connunitv
Development Com. (1987) 196 caI.App'3d 983; fn re Thorner (1975)


File No. I -91- 12 6
Page 3
l- FPPC Ops. 198.) The Act, however, does seek to prevent even the
appearance of a possibLe conflict of interest. 
(witt v. Morrow
(L977 ) 7 O CaI. App. 3d 817, 82 3 . )
fn order to determine whether the foreseeable effect of a
decision is material as to real property in which an official 
has
an interest, the conmission has adopted Regulation La702.3 (copy
enclosed). You have inforrned us that your property is located
more than 2,500 feet from the site of the proposed subdivision.
The effect of a decision will be considered rnaterial vrhen an
official's 
property is rnore than 2,5o0 from a project onlv if:
(1) There are specific circumstances
regarding the decision, its effect, and the nature
of the real property in which the official 
has an
interest, which make it reasonably foreseeabl-e that
the fair market value or the rental value of the
real property in which the official 
has an interest
will be affected by the amounts set forth in
subdivisions (a) (31 (A) or (a) (3) (B)3; and
(2) Either of the following aPPIY:
(A) The effect wiLl not be substantj.ally the
same as the effect upon at Ieast 25 percent of aII
the properties which are within a 2,50o foot radius
of the boundaries of the real property in which the
official 
has an interest; or
(B) There are not at least 10 properties
under separate ownership lrithin a 2,500 foot radius
of the property in which the officia] 
has an
interest.
Regulation 18702.3 (b) .
whether the effect of the decision on your property is positive or
negative is of no consequence under the Act. 
(Younq Advice
Letter. A-89-149, copy enclosed. )
Your participation in decisions on the proposed subdivision
would be permitted unless the value of your property will 
be
affected by the dollar amounts specified in Regulation 18702.3(b)
and there are specific factors which make the impact on your
property somewhat unique. This is a factuaL deter:ninat j-on . In
1" 
subdivisions (a) (3) (A) and (a) (3) (B) provides rhat
decision will have a financial effect of:
(A) Ten thousand dollars (S10,000) or more on the
market value of the real property in which the official
i nterest ,' or
(B) will affect the rental value of the property
or more per 12 month period.
the
fa ir
has an
bv S1, ooo


File No. I-91-L26
Page 4
providing advice the Cor nission does not act as a finder of fact.{
and thus does not evaluate the accuracy of srhatever facts are
presented to us. The information provided in Mr. Tomlinson's
Ietter of l,tarch 12, L99L does not address the factors to be
considered in deterrnining the magnitude of the financial, impact on
your property .
For purposes of determining how your Property may be inpacted
as a result of the decision on the proposed subdivision, v/e draw
your attention to subdivisj.on (d) of Regulation L8702.3. This
subdivision provides:
(d) For a decision which is covered by
subdivision (a) (l) or (b) (1) or (c), factors
which shall be considered in determining
whether the decision wiLL have the effects set
forth in subdivision (a) (3)(A) or (B) include.
but are not linited to:
(1) The proximity of the property which
is the subject of the decision and the
:nagnitude of the proposed project or change in
use in relationship to the property in which
the official 
has an interest,'
(2\ I,lhether it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decisi.on will affect the development
potential or income producing potentia)- of the
property;
(3) In addj.tion to the foregoing, in the
case of residential property, whether it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
resul-t in a change to the character of the
neighborhood incJ.uding, but not limited to,
effects on traffic, 
view, privacy, intensity
of use, noise level-s, air ernissions, or
similar traits of the neighborhood.
Regulation 18702.3 (d) .
After considering these factors, if you conclude that the
proposed subdivision decision wiII not impact your property's fair
market val-ue by Sfo,000 or more, or its rental value by $l.,o0o in
a 12-nonth period, you are perrnitted to participate in the
decisions with respect to the proposed subdivision. If you
conclude that a decision will have an effect which exceeds the
above Iinits, 
you may nevertheJ.ess participate in decisions on the
4 
A= such, any advice provided
that a1I material facts have been
presented. (fn re Oql-esbv, (L9751
is applicable only to the extent
accurately and correctly
1 FPPC Ops. 77, copy enclosed. )


File No. I--97-L26
proposed subdivision provided either at least 25 percent of aLt
the properties within 2,5o0 feet of the subdivision will be
affected in the same manner as your property, or there are less
then ten properties under separate ownership within 2,500 of vour
property . 5
The IPublic Geuerally" Exception
If it is your conclusion that the specified financial impact
and other requisites as provided for in Regulation 18702.3 (b) are
present, a disqualifying conflict v/ould be present. Nevertheless,
you are stil1 permitted to participate in decisions concerning the
proposed subdivision if the effect of the decision on your
interests is not distinguishable from the effect on the pubLic
generally. 
(Section 87103. )
The financial effect of a decision on your property is
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally unless the
decision's effect on your property is substantially the sane as it
is on a1I members of the public or a significant segrnent of the
public. (Regulation 18703; Jorqensen Advice Letter, No. A-90-017;
In re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1,'In re Owen (L976) 2 FPPC Ops.
77. copies enclosed. ) The rrpublic Aenerally" is comprised of the
entire jurisdi,ction of the agency in question. (fn re Leqan,
supra.) Those facts indicating how, and whether, the proposed
subdivision decision will impact all, or a significant segment, of
the city's resj.dents would required assessment. If a
disquall-f y j.ng conf 1ic', exists, you nust determine, based on your
5 
whether or not there are ten or more properties under
separate ownership within 2,500 feet of your property (Regulation
18702,3(b) (2)(B)) is a simple factual determination. As
illustrative 
examples of how Section L8702.3 (b) (2)(A) is applied,
j-f your property was the only parcel within a 2,500 radius that
was suitable for development sj.milar to the proposed subdivision,
you would be required to disqualify yourself frorR participating in
decisions concerning the proposed subdivision because less than
the requisite number of properties within a 2,5oo foot radius of
your property would be affected by the proposed subdivision
decision in a manner sj.nilar to your property. Sinilarly,
disqualification would be required if there were ten parcels
within a 2,500 foot radj.us of your property and only one of the
ten rrould be affected by the proposed subdivision decision in a
manner similar to your property. However, if there were twenty
properties within a 2,500 radius of your property and at least
f j.ve of the twenty would be affected by the proposed subdivision
decision in a manner sinilar to the impact on your property, you
would then be perrnitted to participate in proposed subdivision
decisions.


FiIe No. I -91- 12 6
Page 6
circumstances. $rhether the 'tpublic generallyrr exception vrould
apply to perrnit your participati.on in the subdivision decisions.6
f hope this letter has provided you lrith the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this
natter, or wish to supplenent your advice request lrith additional
information, please contact me at (916) 322-59OL.
Sincerely,
Scott HaIlabrin
Act i
Jona than
Counse I ,
SH : JSR: ken
Enc losures
A" The factors previously discussed in Regulation 18702.3(d) would
be relevant in any consideration of whether the pub).ic Aenera).ly
exception would apply. A group that is large in nurnber and
heterogeneous in quality can constitute a significant segment of
the public for the purposes of the public Aenerally exception.
(In re Ferraro (1978) 4 FPPC ops. 62.) To assist you in
evaluating the tpublic generallyt' exception, I have enclosed
copies of other Conmission advice letters which have addressed the
issue. Enclosed are: West Advice Letter, No. A-89-243; Scher
Advice Letter, No. A-88-479; Flvnn Advice Letter, No. I-88-430.
By
Legal Division
